segments.s029-g113: بیاراست is a past third-person singular verb meaning 'he adorned/arrayed.' آن را is the direct object (آن = 'that/it' + را the definite object marker). The prepositional phrase به دیبای روم ('in/with Roman brocade') follows the object and specifies the material; به here marks the medium/way ('in' or 'with').
segments.s029-g114: ز introduces an instrumental/partitive sense ('with/from'), so ز گوهر بر means 'with jewels upon (it)' where بر is the locative/prepositional 'on/upon.' و links into پیکر از زر بوم, where پیکر 'body/appearance' is qualified by از زر بوم 'of/from golden fabric' (از = 'of/from', بوم here = fabric/canvas), describing the cloth's golden ground.
segments.s029-g115: بزد is a past third-person singular verb 'he struck.' بر سر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his own head' (بر = 'on/upon', سر = 'head', خویش split into خوی + ش here with ش = third‑person possessive 'his'). چون گرد ماه is a simile 'like the round moon' (چون = 'like', گرد = 'round/about', ماه = 'moon').
segments.s029-g116: یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one/a single.' فال فرخ is a noun phrase 'an auspicious/fortunate omen' (فرخ qualifying فال). پی‌افکند is the past form 'he cast/threw' (here 'cast' an omen/lot). شاه at the end is the subject placed post‑verb for emphasis: 'the king cast a single fortunate omen.'
segments.s029-g117: فرو is an adverb 'down/from it' and with هشت ازو ('eight from it') yields 'eight [things] descended/from it.' The following series سرخ و زرد و بنفش simply lists the colors 'red, yellow, and purple' that describe those eight items.
segments.s029-g118: همی is a narrative adverb often signaling continuous or habitual action in past narration ('they would/kept'). خواندش is a past verb meaning 'they called/named it' (the object-marker ـش = 'it'). کاویانی درفش is the name/title 'Kavian standard/banner' and functions as the name they applied: 'they called it the Kaviani banner.'
segments.s029-g119: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/from then on.' هر آن کس که is a free relative 'whoever/anyone who,' and بگرفت is the past form 'seized/took hold (of it),' while گاه ('time/occasion') indicates the timely occasion when this happened. The clause sets the condition for the following action.
segments.s029-g120: به شاهی here means 'to kingship/for kingship' (indicating the role gained). به سر بر نهادی is a layered prepositional phrase meaning 'placed upon the head' (به سر = 'on the head', بر = 'upon', نهادی from نهادن 'to place/put' in past), and کلاه is the object 'hat/cap'—so 'placed the hat on his head to assume kingship.'
segments.s029-g57: An older/poetic word order: the dative pronoun 'بدو' (to him) comes first, then the adverb 'باز' (back/again), followed by the past-tense verb 'دادند' (they gave). 'فرزند او' is the (undemarcated) direct object 'his son' (no modern 'را' here). The subject is omitted and understood as 'they' (3rd plural).
segments.s029-g58: 'به خوبی' is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with kindness' (به used adverbially + خوبی 'goodness/kindness'), placed before the verb. 'بجستند' is past 3rd plural 'they sought', and 'پیوند او' is the object 'his bond/allying' (پیوند = alliance/bond, modified by او). Together: 'they sought his alliance by kindness.'
segments.s029-g59: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he said/commanded' (used by the narrator). 'پس' = 'then'. 'کاوه را' uses the object marker 'را' with Kaveh as the person affected by the command. 'پادشا' is the resultative/complement noun 'king' (i.e., 'he ordered Kaveh to be king' — the causative idea is carried by the construction without an explicit 'کرد').
segments.s029-g60: The conjunction 'که' introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'). 'باشد' is 3rd-singular subjunctive/optative 'may be'/'be', expressing intended state. 'بران' ('upon that' / 'thereon') + 'محضر' ('presence', i.e. the court/audience) with 'اندر' ('in/within') gives 'in that presence', and 'گوا' here means 'witness' — so the clause says he should be there in that court as a witness.
segments.s029-g61: 'چو' = 'when'. The verb is the prefixed verb 'برخواند' (بر + خواند) meaning 'he summoned/called'; here past 3rd-singular with 'کاوه' as subject. 'همه محضرش' = 'all of his presence' i.e. 'his entire court/audience' (همه quantifies محضرش).
segments.s029-g62: 'سبک' is used adverbially here = 'light(ly)' (movement done lightly). 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward'. 'پیران آن کشورش' is the noun phrase 'the elders of that country/realm' (پیران = elders, آن = that, کشورش = 'his country' or 'that country's' with possessive suffix). The prepositional phrase modifies the movement of the court.
segments.s029-g63: 'خروشید' is an intransitive past-form meaning 'a roar/cries rose' or 'they cried out'. 'کای' is a vocative interjection here ('O!') rather than the royal title. 'پای مردان دیو' is a genitive phrase 'the foot/force of the demon-men' (پای used figuratively as 'force' or 'troop'), which is the content of the outcry.
segments.s029-g64: 'بریده دل' is a fixed/poetic description meaning 'having the heart cut' i.e. terrified. 'از ترس' = 'from/because of fear of'. 'گیهان خدیو' is a compound genitive 'the world's khedive' (گیهان = the world; خدیو = khedive/ruler), giving the cause: hearts were struck with fear of the sovereign of the world.
segments.s029-g105: همی رفت is a past continuous/progressive construction ('kept on going' / 'went on'). پیش اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward the inner quarters' (پیش = 'in the presence of / toward', اندرون = 'inner apartments'). مرد گرد uses گرد in the sense of a circle/collection ('a circle/assembly of men'), so the whole segment means 'he proceeded toward the inner quarters of the circle of men.'
segments.s029-g106: جهانی برو is a descriptive phrase: جهانی = 'worldly' and برو (literally 'one who goes') gives the sense 'a worldly rover/one who goes about the world.' انجمن شد is past 'joined/entered the assembly' (شد = became/entered). نه خرد negates the following quality: 'not of wisdom' = 'not wise.' The order is poetic (adjective + agent, then verb, then negative attribute).
segments.s029-g107: بدانست is a simple past 'knew it.' خود کافریدون is an emphatic subject construction ('Kafridun himself'). کجاست is an embedded interrogative/indirect question 'where (it) is' — the present-tense copula form appears inside the reported clause. Word order places the embedded question after the verb 'knew.'
segments.s029-g108: سر اندر کشید is an idiomatic past action 'he drew his head in / withdrew his head' (سر = 'head', اندر = 'in/within', کشید = 'drew/pulled' here). و links clauses. همی رفت راست: همی رفت again shows past continuous 'kept going / went on' and راست is an adverb 'straight/direct,' so 'and he went straight on.'
segments.s029-g109: بیامد is the past 'came.' به درگاه سالار نو is a prepositional phrase 'to the court/threshold of the new lord' (درگاه = royal court/presence, سالار = lord/commander, نو = 'new'). The whole segment locates the arrival.
segments.s029-g110: بدیدندش is the past with an object clitic 'they saw him' (بدیدند + ش). آن جا = 'there.' و برخاست غو is a poetic inversion meaning 'and uproar arose' (برخاست = 'arose/rose', غو = 'uproar'), where the verb precedes the noun for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s029-g111: چو ... دید کی is a temporal complex: چو = 'when,' followed by the object clause 'that skin on the spear' (آن پوست بر نیزه), then دید = 'saw.' The final کی here functions as 'then/when' in classical usage, completing the temporal clause: 'when he saw that skin on the spear.'
segments.s029-g112: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'by/with goodness' or 'fortunately/well.' یکی اختر = 'one star' (numeric + noun). افگند is a past tense 'threw/fell' (here 'fell' or 'was cast'); پی after it means 'after/behind' — 'one star fell behind/after [it].' The clause is compact and poetic: adverbial + subject + verb + directional adverb.
segments.s029-g129: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو 'when'. Subject (فریدون) comes first; the clause ends with the past-tense verb دید 'saw', a typical SOV order in Persian. بر آن گونه is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('in/on that manner' = 'thus') modifying what he saw.
segments.s029-g130: Main clause: جهان is the topical/accusative object 'the world', followed by the locative/presence phrase پیش ضحاک ('in the presence of / before Zahhak'), then the predicate adjective وارونه 'inverted/overturned' and the past verb دید 'he saw'. The clause literally reads 'he saw the world, before Zahhak, overturned.'
segments.s029-g131: سوی مادر آمد is a simple clause 'he came toward (his) mother' with آمد (past 'came') final. The following short phrase کمر بر میان describes his bearing/equipment: کمر here refers to the waist/girdle and بر میان ('on the middle') specifies its position—an adjectival/prepositional phrase attached to the clause.
segments.s029-g132: This is a participial/adverbial clause describing a concurrent action: به سر (on the head) + بر (on) + نهاده (past participle 'placed') — 'having placed on (his) head' — followed by کلاه کیان, a genitive construction 'the crown/hat of the Kians' (i.e., the royal crown). The word order is slightly poetic but standard for participial modifiers.
segments.s029-g133: که introduces the content/explanatory clause 'that/because': من (I) + رفتنی‌ام is a predicative adjective/auxiliary construction meaning 'I am about to go / I am going to depart' (رفتنی indicates imminent departure) and سوی کارزار is the directional phrase 'toward the battlefield'. Verbally the clause ends with the predicate 'رفتنی‌ام'.
segments.s029-g134: This is an optative/wish sentence addressed to the addressee: تو را ('for you' with را as topical/emphatic marker) جز نیایش ('except prayer') followed by مباد, an old negative optative meaning 'may it not be / may there not be', and the emphatic ایچ کار ('any/any other deed' = 'no affair at all'). Together: 'May there be nothing for you except prayer.'
segments.s029-g135: ز here is the poetic preposition 'away from / from' giving an imperative/admonitive sense: 'leave the world' or 'away from the world'. جهان‌آفرین را is the marked direct object 'the world's Creator' (را marks definiteness/emphasis) and پرست is used here as a verb-like exhortation 'worship' (an archaic/poetic form related to پرستیدن). The phrase roughly commands: 'Leave the world; worship the Creator of the world.'
segments.s029-g136: ازو دان is an admonitive clause: ازو 'from him/of him' + دان (imperative/infinitive 'know/consider') = 'learn/know from Him'. بهر نیکی is a purpose phrase 'for the sake of goodness', and زور دست is a noun phrase 'strength (and) hand' (i.e. power/force and the hand that acts). The whole clause says: 'Know from Him that strength and (the) hand are for goodness' — i.e., use power for good deeds.
segments.s029-g121: بر is the preposition 'upon/on' introducing a locative phrase; آن is a demonstrative 'that' modifying the following noun phrase; بی‌بها is an adjective placed before چرم and here describes the leather as without (ordinary) price or value (an older/poetic adjective); چرم آهنگران is a genitive construction 'the leather of the blacksmiths' with the possessor آهنگران following the head noun, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s029-g122: برآویختی is the past of برآویختن 'to hang/attach' (an epic/poetic past form; in Ferdowsi such endings may be ambiguous between persons but function here as the narrative past); نو به نو is an adverbial reduplication meaning 'one by one' / 'anew, one after another' with به marking the adverbial relation; گوهران is the (definite) object 'gems' placed after the adverbial phrase — Persian tendency is SOV, so the verb comes at the end of the clause, and the object is linked directly to that verb.
segments.s029-g123: ز (a poetic form of از) means 'from' and introduces the material/source; دیبای is 'brocade/silk' and is modified by the adjective پرمایه 'full-bodied, rich' placed after it in this phrase (an attributive adjective); و links the two types of garment to the following noun پرنیان 'silken robe' (here used as another luxurious textile item).
segments.s029-g124: بر آن گونه is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning 'in that manner/guise' (بر + demonstrative + گونه = 'in that way'); شد is the past of شدن 'became/rose' and functions here as the main verb; اختر کاویان is a nominal phrase 'the star (fortune) of the Kayanians' with اختر (star) as head and کاویان (the Kayanian dynasty / their rulers) as a genitive modifier — together 'the Kayanian star' rose/appeared in that way.
segments.s029-g125: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'; اندر تیره شب is the poetic order 'in the dark night' (اندر = in); خورشید بود places the predicate 'there was a sun' with بود as the past copula 'was' — the clause asserts that even in dark times a sun existed (i.e. a shining hope).
segments.s029-g126: جهان را uses را as the topical/definite object marker emphasizing 'the world'; ازو is a prepositional pronominal 'from him/it' (referring back to the sun or its agent); دل پر امید بود is a stative clause 'the heart was full of hope' where پر is the predicative adjective 'full (of)' modifying دل and بود is the past copula 'was' — syntactically the phrase says the world's heart, because of him, was full of hope.
segments.s029-g127: بگشت is a past verb meaning 'returned/turned back' (an older narrative past form); اندرین 'in these (things/times)' locates the action; نیز 'also/again' adds that this return was another occurrence; چندی here means 'for a while' and scopes over جهان 'the world' (the noun placed at the end of the phrase as topic).
segments.s029-g128: همی is an emphatic/continuative adverb 'still/continually'; بودنی is a verbal noun 'being/existence' functioning as the object of داشت 'had' (i.e. 'had existence' = 'continued to exist'); اندر نهان is a locative phrase 'in secret/hidden within' specifying where that existence persisted — overall a compact classical structure meaning 'it still possessed hidden existence'.
segments.s029-g89: چو is a classical temporal conjunction meaning "when." Word order is conjunction + subject (کاوه) + adverb (برون) + verbal predicate (شد) + prepositional phrase (ز درگاه شاه). برون شد is the past action 'went out/issued forth' formed with شدن; ز before درگاه is the poetic preposition 'from' and درگاه شاه means 'the king's court/presence.'
segments.s029-g90: This clause has an inverted, compact word order: an adverbial or exclamatory برو (here used colloquially/poetically as a 'thereupon/straightaway' marker rather than a literal imperative) + predicate گشت 'became/turned' + predicate-complement انجمن 'an assembly' + subject/locative بازارگاه 'the marketplace'. گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became/turned into.'
segments.s029-g91: همی is a classical adverb marking continuous or immediate action ('was/kept on'). بر is the verbal prefix in the compound verb برخروشید (here separated), and خروشید is the past form of خروشیدن 'to roar/cry out.' فریاد خواند is a light-verb construction: the noun فریاد ('a cry') combined with خواند (here 'called/ cried') to mean 'he cried out.'
segments.s029-g92: جهان را uses را as the definite/direct-object/topic marker marking 'the world' as the target; سراسر سوی is an adverbial phrase 'throughout/in every direction.' داد is a noun meaning 'justice' and خواند again is the light verb 'he called,' so داد خواند = 'he cried/called for justice.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s029-g93: این relative phrase opens with از آن چرم 'from that leather.' کآهنگران is the fused form of که آهنگران ('which/that blacksmiths') introducing a relative clause; پشت پای literally 'the back of the foot' (پشت + پای) is the locus phrase. Classical Persian often compresses the relative marker and noun as here.
segments.s029-g94: بپوشند is the present/subjunctive/habitual form of پوشیدن used in a relative/habitual sense 'that (they) wear.' هنگام 'at the time of' modifies the following noun زخم 'a wound.' درای here functions as a descriptive/adjectival word 'having/possessing' (i.e. 'when having a wound' or 'when wounded'). The clause describes customary use: 'which blacksmiths wear on the back of the foot when wounded.'
segments.s029-g95: همان is a demonstrative/emphatic 'that very' or 'the same'; it emphasizes the identity of کاوه. آن is a further demonstrative 'that.' بر سر نیزه کردن is a fixed verb phrase 'to place on the head/top of a spear'; کرد is the light verb 'did/caused' used to form the action 'set/placed.' The construction is subject + object + prepositional phrase + کرد.
segments.s029-g96: همان‌گه is a temporal adverb 'at that instant/thereupon.' ز بازار is the prepositional phrase 'from the market.' برخاست is the past of برخاستن 'rose/departed.' گرد here is an adverb meaning 'around/about' (it often follows the verb in classical diction to indicate movement around or going about). The clause describes immediate movement: 'thereupon he rose from the market and went about.'
segments.s029-g33: بر آن محضر اژدها is a locative prepositional phrase: بر آن = 'at/ before that', محضر اژدها = 'the presence/audience of the dragon' (محضر takes a genitive noun). ناگزیر is an adverbial/participial modifier placed after the place-phrase meaning 'compelled/reluctantly', qualifying the action that follows.
segments.s029-g34: گواهی نوشتند is a past-tense verbal clause (نوشتند = 'they wrote/inscribed'); گواهی (object) precedes the verb in the classical style. برنا و پیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'the young and the old' naming the agents; the plural sense is shown by the verb نوشتند and the nouns follow the verb as postverbal subjects/apposition.
segments.s029-g35: هم آنگه یکایک is a sequence of adverbials: هم آنگه 'then/at that moment' and یکایک 'one by one, each in turn'. ز درگاه شاه is a locative/source phrase 'from the king's court' (ز = 'from', درگاه = 'royal court', شاه = 'king'). These adverbials set time and source before the verb.
segments.s029-g36: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن here appears as a verbal noun/infinitive used adverbially ('crying aloud' / 'roaring'), and دادخواه names who acted ('the seeker of justice' — appositive/agent following the verb). The sequence yields 'they arose, crying, the justice-seekers.'
segments.s029-g37: ستم دیده is a compound noun meaning 'the wronged/oppressed person' (دیده here is a past-participial use). را is the definite/direct-object marker attaching to that noun. پیش او = 'before him / in his presence'. خواندند is a past plural verb here meaning 'they summoned/called' (or 'they read/recited' depending on context); together the clause means they brought/summoned the wronged person before the ruler.
segments.s029-g38: بر نامدارانش is a prepositional phrase 'among/into his notables' (بر here marks placement relative to the group's members). بنشاندند is the past plural transitive verb 'they seated/placed' — the object ('the wronged one') is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s029-g39: بدو = 'to him' (dative), گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' (the senses list did not include 'to say' so it is marked below). مهتر = 'the chief' (subject) follows the verb, a common classical order. به روی دژم is a manner phrase 'with a sullen/grim face' (به ... روی = 'with a ... face'), describing how he spoke.
segments.s029-g40: که at the start introduces the reported speech/command ('that' / 'and then'). بر گوی is an instruction 'speak out' (بر as a prefix/particle with گوی 'say'), تا is a connective particle here introducing the ensuing question, and از که دیدی ستم؟ is the direct question 'from whom did you experience/suffer injustice?' (دیدی is the past second-person form 'did you see/experience').
segments.s029-g49: شماریت is شمار (count/reckoning) plus the possessive/second-person suffix -ت, so 'your reckoning'. با من is a prepositional phrase ('with me') placed after the noun. بباید گرفت is a modal construction: باید ('must') with the verbal root گرفت ('take/seize'), forming a necessity passive/impersonal reading 'must be taken' — the verb phrase comes at the end in typical Persian word order.
segments.s029-g50: بدان is be- + دان (imperative/imperatival form 'know' / 'consider it'); the prefix ب- here forms the verb. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'). جهان ماند اندر شگفت is subject + verb + locative phrase: جهان 'the world', ماند 'remain/last', اندر شگفت 'in astonishment/wonder' — شگفت is a noun here ('wonder') and اندر is the locative preposition 'in'.
segments.s029-g51: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional sense ('unless' / 'except that'); کز is the fused form of که + از meaning 'from' ('from which' / 'from'); شمار تو 'your reckoning' (شمار + تو) is the source phrase; آید پدید is a verbal cluster: آید ('comes') + پدید ('to light/appear'), giving 'comes to light' or 'appears'.
segments.s029-g52: که is the subordinating 'that' linking the appearance to its content. نوبت 'turn' (one's turn or allotment) is the subject; ز گیتی 'from the world' (ز = from, poetic form); به من 'to me' (dative); چون رسید 'چون' here = 'as/when' and رسید = 'reached/arrived', so the clause means 'that the turn of the world has reached me.'
segments.s029-g53: که introduces the content clause. مارانت is ماران ('snakes') with the possessive -ت 'your'; را after مارانت functions as an object/topic marker highlighting the indirect recipients ('to/for your snakes'). مغز فرزند من is the noun phrase 'the brain of my child' (مغز = brain, فرزند من = my child) and is the theme being given — Persian permits this topicalization/word-order inversion for emphasis.
segments.s029-g54: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle here ('indeed/always'). داد is the verb 'gave' / 'give' (here best read in a passive/necessitative paraphrase with باید). باید 'must' expresses necessity; ز هر انجمن 'from/at every assembly' (ز = from/at in poetic use, هر = every, انجمن = assembly). Together the phrase yields 'must (indeed) be given at every assembly.'
segments.s029-g55: سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). به گفتار او is a prepositional phrase 'to/at his speech/words' (به = towards/at, گفتار = speech, او = his). بنگرید is a past/simple/formal verb 'he looked/gazed/regarded' (third-person past), so the whole clause means 'the spahbed looked upon/listened to his speech.'
segments.s029-g56: شگفت آمدش is an impersonal/experiential phrase 'wonder came to him' (شگفت = wonder/marvel, آمدش = 'came to him'). کآن here introduces the subordinate clause 'that/those' before سخن‌ها شنید; however the provided lexeme senses for کآن do not include the usual demonstrative 'that/those', so its sense is treated separately. سخن‌ها 'the words' (plural) and شنید 'he heard' complete the clause: 'he was astonished when he heard those words.'
segments.s029-g73: Word order: subject (مهان, “the guests”) comes first, then the object marked with را (شاه را, “the king” as definite object), then the past plural verb خواندند (“they cried/called out”). آفرین is an exclamation placed after the verb and here represents the shouted word. را is the standard Persian definite/object marker; خواندند in this context means ‘called out’ or ‘shouted’ rather than ‘read.’
segments.s029-g74: که‌ای here is an archaic/poetic vocative particle (an addressed exclamation, roughly ‘O’). نامور شهریار زمین is a compact title: نامور modifies شهریار, and زمین (without an explicit ezâfe in this poetic line) qualifies شهریار as ‘of the earth’ (i.e. ‘sovereign of the earth’). The whole segment is the vocative that follows the shouted آفرین.
segments.s029-g75: This is a prepositional/source phrase: ز + چرخ فلک = “from the wheel/sphere of heaven.” بر سرت is the prepositional phrase “upon your head.” باد سرد literally ‘cold wind’; here باد is the noun ‘wind’ and سرد modifies it. The finite verb (expressing the wish/optative) appears in the next segment.
segments.s029-g76: نیارد is a 3rd-person negative optative/subjunctive here (‘may ... not come/bring’); it governs the verbal noun/infinitive گذشتن (‘passing’), so the clause expresses a wish that a cold wind not come upon you while you pass. به‌روز is a poetic locative/genitive ‘of the day’ and نبرد is the noun ‘battle,’ so گذشتن به‌روز نبرد = ‘passing through/over the day of battle.’
segments.s029-g77: چرا is the interrogative ‘why.’ پیش تو is a locative phrase ‘in your presence / before you.’ کاوه is the subject (proper name) and خام‌گوی is an epithet ‘plain‑spoken/blunt,’ attached to کاوه; in poetry the linking ezâfe is often omitted, so the adjective follows the name.
segments.s029-g78: به سان (‘in the manner of’ / ‘like’) introduces a simile; همالان is the comparison class ‘porters/carriers’ (here figurative: lowly attendants), and کند is the 3rd‑person singular present of کردن ‘does/acts.’ سرخ روی is the result phrase ‘red‑faced’ (سرخ modifies روی ‘face’); word order and omission of ezâfe are typical of poetic diction.
segments.s029-g79: همه is a collective ‘all/everyone.’ محضر ما = ‘our presence / our court/audience’ (محضر = the presence/audience of the ruler) and پیمان تو = ‘your covenant/pledge.’ The two nouns are joined by و (‘and’) to list what is affected in the next clause.
segments.s029-g80: بپیچد (from پیچیدن) is used figuratively here as ‘be turned/whirled’ or ‘become twisted/ruined’; it takes بدرد (here used idiomatically as ‘to (someone’s) harm / to cause hurt’) so the phrase بدرد بپیچد means ‘be turned to harm’ or ‘go to ruin.’ ز فرمان تو = ‘because of/by your command’ (ز = ‘by/from’). Grammatically, the verb بپیچد governs the preceding noun phrase as its affected theme.
segments.s029-g1: چنان بد که is a fixed poetic clause meaning “so (it was) that…” (چنان = “so/thus”, بد here functions as an adjective intensifier). ضحاک را uses را as the definite/accusative marker attached to the name Zahhak. روز و شب is an adverbial phrase “day and night” placed after the marked object; Persian poetry allows flexible order, so the temporal phrase appears between the object and the following clause.
segments.s029-g2: به نام فریدون is a prepositional phrase meaning “in the name of Fereydun” (به + نام). گشادی is a verbal/participial form meaning “opened” or “uttered” (poetic past), followed by its object دو لب “two lips” (literally ‘two lips’ = ‘his mouth’ or ‘he spoke’). Word order here is typical of poetry: prepositional phrase + verb + object.
segments.s029-g3: بر آن برز بالا is a locative phrase: بر “on/upon” + آن “that” + برز بالا “high height/lofty place” (برز as ‘height’, بالا as an attributive ‘high’). ز بیم نشیب uses ز “from/of” + بیم “fear” + نشیب “descent/fall,” literally “from fear of a fall.” The whole segment sets the place and the cause (fear) for what follows.
segments.s029-g4: شده is the perfect/passive participle meaning “has become” or simply “became.” ز before آفریدون here is causal “because of” (z + noun), so شده ز آفریدون = “became because of Fereydun.” دلش is دل + enclitic pronoun ش “his,” and پر نهیب is the predicate “full (پر) of نهیب,” where نهیب is a strong cry/rebuke; the phrase means his heart was filled with a thunderous cry or reproach.
segments.s029-g5: چنان is an adverb meaning “so”/“thus.” Here چنان بد که is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic/resultive phrase meaning “it so happened that / so much so that.” یک روز is an adverbial time phrase “one day.” بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase with بر + noun meaning “on an ivory throne” (تخت ‘throne’, عاج ‘ivory’); the preposition precedes the noun as expected in classical Persian word order.
segments.s029-g6: نهاده is the perfective/past participle of نهادن “to place/put” and functions as the main verbal action “(he) placed.” به سر marks the target of placement “on (his) head” (به + سر). The following phrase بر ز پیروزه تاج is a poetic/inverted description of the object: پیروزه modifies تاج (“turquoise crown”), while the preposition ز (poetic ‘of/from’) links the material/quality; تاج (the noun) comes at the end for poetic weight. The overall order is literary inversion but reads: “he placed on his head a crown of turquoise.”
segments.s029-g7: ز هر کشوری is a prepositional/genitive phrase meaning “from every country/land” (ز = poetic ‘from’, هر = ‘every’, کشوری = ‘of the country/realm’). مهتران را is the definite/plural object (مهتران ‘chiefs/elders/leaders’, را = object marker). بخواست is a past tense verb ‘he requested/asked for/summoned’ (3rd‑person past). The implied subject is the king; classical Persian often leaves the subject unstated.
segments.s029-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result “so that/that.” در پادشاهی is “in (his) kingship/reign” (در = in, پادشاهی = kingship/rule). کند is the present/subjunctive/optative form of کردن “to do/make” used here as a purposive/modal verb “might do / should do.” پشت راست is an idiomatic phrase: پشت originally “back/support” and راست “straight/firm”; together پشت راست کردن means to make the support/backing secure — i.e., to set the monarchy or rule on a firm footing. Grammatically, the clause states the purpose of summoning the chiefs.
segments.s029-g81: This is a short clause meaning “who answered quickly.” کی is the interrogative “who” placed before the epithet نامور (“the famed/renowned one”). پاسخ آورد is an idiomatic phrase: پاسخ (object) + آورد (past of آوردن) = “gave an answer / replied.” زود is an adverb (“quickly/soon”) modifying آورد and comes after the verb, as is common in older Persian word order.
segments.s029-g82: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause here (roughly “for” / “that”). از من is a prepositional phrase “from me / by me.” شگفتی is a noun meaning “marvel, wonder.” بباید is the modal “must/ought” and is followed by شنود, an older third-person form (subjunctive/optative/present) of شنیدن used here to mean “shall/must hear” — together conveying “he must/shall hear.”
segments.s029-g83: که begins this causal/time clause (“for/that/when”). چون here functions as a temporal conjunction “when / as.” کاوه is the proper name Kâveh. آمد is the past tense “came.” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” and درگه is the royal court; the word پدید (an archaic/postpositive verbal/adjectival form meaning “appeared/manifest”) follows the verb phrase, giving a slightly elevated or archaic word order: “came forth from the court, appearing.”
segments.s029-g84: This is a simple SOV clause in older Persian order: دو گوش من (“my two ears”) is the subject (noun + possessor after the noun), آواز او را is the definite object marked by را (“his voice”), and شنید is the past-tense verb “heard.” The doubling of دو (two) is emphatic — “both my ears heard.”
segments.s029-g85: میان governs the following two nouns and means “between”; thus میان من و او = “between me and him.” ز is again the poetic “from,” ایوان is the iwan/porch of the court. درست here is an adverb meaning “straight/just/exactly” and modifies the spatial phrase (i.e., “directly from the iwan”). The phrase sets the spatial relation for the reported speech that follows.
segments.s029-g86: تو گفتی is a reporting clause “you said” (گفتی = second-person past of گفتن). یکی is an indefinite “one / a certain,” introducing کوه آهن “an iron mountain.” برست is an archaic past form used in epic/poetic narrative meaning roughly “arose/appeared / burst forth” here; together the clause reports the quoted assertion: “You said, ‘a mountain of iron arose.’”
segments.s029-g87: ندانم is the negated present of دانستن: ن‑ (negative) + دانم (“I know”) = “I do not know.” چه ... شاید is a verbless exclamatory/indirect question construction meaning “what may (happen) / what will be” with شاید as the modal “perhaps / it is possible.” بدن here means “body” or “fate/condition of the body” in context, and سپس means “after/thereupon.” The sequence expresses uncertainty about what will become of the body after this event.
segments.s029-g88: که introduces the explanatory clause “for / because.” راز سپهری is a genitive phrase “the secret of the heavens/celestial secret” (سپهری = celestial/pertaining to the heavens). ندانست is the past tense negative “knew not / did not know.” کس here functions negatively (in classical Persian word order after the verb) meaning “no one.” Together: “for no one knew the celestial secret.”
segments.s029-g41: خروشید and زد are two past-tense, third-person singular verbs in sequence (he cried out / and struck). دست بر سر is the object phrase '(his) hand upon (his) head' (an idiom for striking one's head in grief); the possessor is understood from context. ز شاه uses the archaic/poetic preposition ز (here meaning 'because of / on account of / in the presence of the king'), linking the lamenting gesture to the king.
segments.s029-g42: که introduces the quoted words (roughly 'saying that' / introduces direct speech). شاها is a vocative 'O king!' (archaic/formal). منم is a fused form meaning 'I am' (من + ام, copula implicit elsewhere). کاوه دادخواه is apposition: the personal name Kaweh followed by the epithet 'seeker/avenger of justice'.
segments.s029-g43: یکی 'a certain / a single' gives an indefinite nuance; بی‌زیان is an adjective 'harmless / innocent'; مرد 'man' is the noun. آهنگرم is آهنگر (blacksmith) plus the 1st-person suffix -م, functioning here as a verbless predicate 'I am a blacksmith' (the suffix -م supplies the 'am' in classical narrative).
segments.s029-g44: ز شاه uses the poetic preposition ز = 'because of / on account of / from (the king)'. آتش آید همی is a present/habitual sense ('fire comes/is coming continuously') with آید (3rd person singular present) and the adverbial همی 'still / continually'. بر سرم literally 'upon my head' (بر = on; سرم = 'my head' with the possessive -م); here it means 'falls upon me' or 'presses upon me'.
segments.s029-g45: تو شاهی is a verbless nominal sentence: 'you are king' (شاهی = kingship/regal status used predicatively, copula omitted). و‌گر is the conditional conjunction 'if / but if'. اژدها پیکری is an adjectival phrase 'dragon-bodied' or 'having the body/shape of a dragon' (پیکری attaches to اژدها to indicate form/appearance).
segments.s029-g46: بباید is an impersonal/modal form 'it must / one must / ought to' (necessitative). بدین here means 'in/for this' (به + این → 'with/in this'); داستان is 'the matter / this tale / this case' and داوری is the noun 'judgment / adjudication'. The whole string means 'one must give judgment in this affair.' Note: the tokenization in the word list splits بدین oddly; treat بدین as the prepositional phrase 'by/with this'.
segments.s029-g47: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. هفت کشور 'seven countries/realms' is the subject phrase. به شاهی تو is a prepositional phrase 'to/under your kingship' (به + شاهی + تو = 'under your rule'). راست at the end is the predicate/adjective 'true / indeed' — the clause reads 'if the seven realms are truly under your kingship.'
segments.s029-g48: چرا 'why' introduces the rhetorical question. رنج و سختی 'suffering and hardship' are coordinated nouns; همه 'all' scopes over them. بهر ماست = بهر (for/to the benefit of / to the account of) + ما (us) + ست (is) yields 'all is for us' meaning 'falls upon us / affects us' — idiomatically 'why is all the suffering upon us?'.
segments.s029-g9: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'from/after that' (preposition + demonstrative + postposition). چنین گفت is a verb phrase 'thus said' with گفت the past tense 3rd-person verb placed after the adverbial; با موبدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the mobeds' (با + plural noun) marking the addressees.
segments.s029-g10: که‌ای here introduces the quoted or vocative address; ای is the vocative particle 'O' (poetic), following or fused with که. پرهنر and باگهر are adjectival modifiers placed before the noun بخردان, giving two qualities (learned/cultured; jewel‑adorned/precious). بخردان is a plural/collective vocative 'wise ones' (بـ- خِرَد + -ان) addressed by the speaker.
segments.s029-g11: The clause uses a reversed existential/possessive construction: مرا (to me / of me) functions like an indirect object ('for me' / 'I have'), در نهانی is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' (در + نهانی), یکی is an indefinite 'a certain/one', and دشمن‌ست is the predicate ('is an enemy') with the copula‑verb at the end (ست = است). Word order places the possessee/affected party (مرا) early and the copula at the end, as typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s029-g12: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause 'which/and this', بر بخردان literally 'upon/to the wise' (بر used to mark the audience/recipient here), این سخن روشن است is a simple copular clause 'this speech/matter is clear' with روشن as predicate adjective and است (copula) final.
segments.s029-g13: به سال اندکی is an idiom 'little in years / young' formed with به + noun + adjective; و به دانش بزرگ parallels it 'and great in learning' (به + noun + adjective). The preposition به introduces the domain (age, learning) in which the quality holds.
segments.s029-g14: گوی is a poetic present/impersonal 'they say / call' introducing an epithet; بدنژادی is the noun/adjective 'low‑born' modifying the person so described; دلیر و سترگ are two coordinating adjectives 'brave and (archaically) great/mighty' applied as the epithets. The verb گوی introduces reported naming; adjectives follow the named noun phrase.
segments.s029-g15: اگر چه is the concessive conjunction 'although/though'. به سال اندک repeats the 'young in years' idiom (به + سال + اندک). ای راستان is a vocative 'O righteous ones' (ای vocative + راستان 'the upright/righteous'), addressing listeners directly; the vocative interrupting the clause is typical in epic/poetic speech.
segments.s029-g16: درین کار = در + این (fused as درین) 'in this affair'; موبد is the agent 'the mobed (priest)'. زدش داستان is an idiomatic sequence: زد (past of زدن) plus the clitic -ش 'him' and direct object داستان 'a story'; together it means 'struck him with a story' i.e. 'told him a tale' — note the clitic object attaches to the verb and the copular/lexical verb (زد) appears at the end as usual.
segments.s029-g25: بباید is an impersonal/modal verb meaning 'one must/ought to' (classical Persian usage). The phrase بدین بود is literally 'be in/with this' (به + این in fused form) followed by the copula بود; together بدین بود هم‌داستان = 'be of the same mind about this.' Word order places the modal بباید before the predicative phrase.
segments.s029-g26: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for / that'). من is the first‑person subject. ناشکیبم is the adjective ناشکیب 'impatient' with the enclitic -م ‘I am’ (present/state). بدین داستان repeats the locative/contrastive phrase 'with this tale/story' (به+این + داستان). The clause explains the reason for the previous statement.
segments.s029-g27: یکی here functions as 'a certain / someone' (indefinite) modifying the following noun. محضر is the location/occasion 'presence / court' (i.e., in front of the ruler or in the official record). اکنون is the adverb 'now'. بباید + نوشت forms an obligation: 'one must write' — نوشت is the verbal stem used here as the act to be done.
segments.s029-g28: که introduces the content of what should be written ('that ...'). جز = 'except'. تخم نیکی = 'the seed / offspring of goodness' (تخم here = 'seed/offspring', نیکی = 'goodness' or 'good deeds/lineage'). سپهبد is the agent/title. نکشت in the verse is the past negative construction (ن + کشت) meaning 'did not kill' — the dataset entry for نکشت is a mismatched lemma; here it is a negative past verb meaning 'killed not / did not slay'.
segments.s029-g29: نگوید here functions as an admonitive/impersonal jussive ('let no one speak' / 'do not speak') with سخن ('word / speech') as its object. جز همه راستی = 'except all/complete truth' (همه qualifying راستی as 'whole/entire truth'). Word order places the prohibition/admonition before the restriction 'except complete truth.'
segments.s029-g30: نخواهد is the impersonal/future negative sense 'there will not be' (classical use of خواهد). به داد اندرون is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'with regard to justice within / to the internal administration of justice.' کاستی is the noun 'lack/deficiency.' Together: 'There will be no deficiency in justice within.'
segments.s029-g31: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. بیم سپهبد is a genitive phrase 'the fear of the spahbed.' همه راستان is the noun phrase 'all the righteous' (همه = 'all', راستان = 'the righteous/ upright ones') serving as the subject of the next verb.
segments.s029-g32: بر آن کار = 'to/onto that work/affair' (بر + demonstrative آن + noun کار). گشتند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they turned / became' (گشت + -ند). هم‌داستان functions adverbially/adjectivally: 'in agreement / of one mind.' The whole phrase says that they turned to that task unitedly.
segments.s029-g137: فرو is an adverbial particle meaning 'down/away' placed before the verb; ریخت is the past tense of ریختن ('to pour/flow') so فرو ریخت = 'poured down'. آب is the thing that poured (tears) and appears after the verb by poetic inversion. از مژه مادرش is a prepositional phrase 'from his mother's eyelash' (مژه 'eyelash/eye', مادر 'mother') with the enclitic ش = 'his'.
segments.s029-g138: همی + past verb خواند gives a classical continuous/habitual sense ('kept calling/crying out'). با خون دل is an idiomatic instrumental phrase 'with the blood of his heart' = 'with a bleeding/aching heart' (deep sorrow). داورش is داور ('judge') with the possessive enclitic -ش = 'his Judge' — here a divine/addressee sense.
segments.s029-g139: به یزدان همی گفت = 'he said to God' with همی giving the iterative/continuous tone common in epic Persian. زنهار is an exclamation 'take heed!/I warn you' (here functioning as the content of what is said). من is the speaker's 'I' attached to the exclamation.
segments.s029-g140: سپردم is the past tense 'I entrusted/handed over'. تو را contains the pronoun تو 'you/him' plus the object marker را (here marking the person entrusted). ای جهاندار من is a vocative phrase 'O ruler/sovereign of the world, my ...' addressing God; the vocative clarifies the addressee of the entrusting.
segments.s029-g141: بگردان is an imperative (2nd person) 'turn away/remove'. ز جانش = 'from his life' (ز = 'from', جانش = جان + enclitic ش = 'his life'). بد جاودان = 'evil/ill that is everlasting' (جاودان 'eternal'); poetic word order places modifiers succinctly.
segments.s029-g142: بپرداز is another imperative here meaning 'deliver/save/spare' (addressing the divine). گیتی is the object 'the world'. ز نابخردان is a prepositional phrase 'from the foolish' (نابخردان plural). The verb precedes its object and the ز-phrase marks the source/agent to be removed.
segments.s029-g143: فریدون is the subject. سبک ساز رفتن is a verbal-nominal sequence meaning 'to make light (one's baggage) and go' — سبک 'light (in weight)' + ساز as a formative 'making/doing' with رفتن 'to go': together 'prepare to depart lightly'. گرفت (from گرفتن) functions here as a light auxiliary meaning 'began to' or 'took to (doing)'.
segments.s029-g144: سخن را marks 'the speech/words' as definite object with را. ز هر کس = 'from every person' (= from everyone). نهفتن گرفت combines the infinitive 'to hide/withhold' (نهفتن) with گرفت as auxiliary 'began to' — hence 'he began to keep his words hidden from everyone.'
segments.s029-g97: خروشان is an adverbial participle meaning “roaring” or “shouting”; همی is the old continuous/frequentive particle (here marking the ongoing or habitual sense of the verb); رفت is the past simple “went.” نیزه به دست is an instrumental/possession phrase “spear in (his) hand” (به + دست = “in/at the hand”), placed after the verb in typical Persian poetic word order.
segments.s029-g98: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory particle introducing a direct address (archaic poetic usage: “O ...!”). نامداران is the plural adjective/noun “renowned” or “illustrious ones,” and یزدان‌پرست is a compound noun “God‑worshipper / pious (one).” The whole segment is an exclamation addressed to that group.
segments.s029-g99: کسی introduces an indefinite subject “someone / anyone.” کاو is an archaic relative/particle here meaning “who” (classical usage) rather than its literal lexical senses listed; it links that someone to the verbal clause that follows. هوای فریدون کند is a verbal phrase “has a desire for Fereydun / longs for Fereydun,” with هوای (desire) governing the person/name.
segments.s029-g100: دل از بند ضحاک بیرون کند is a clause expressing the desired/resulting action: دل (heart/mind) + از بند (from the fetter/bond) + ضحاک (proper name) + بیرون کند (“may (he) bring (it) out” / “free (it)”). کند is the present/subjunctive/hortative verbal form used here to express a wish or general statement about what such a person will/do or should do.
segments.s029-g101: بپویید is an imperative/hortative plural (“seek/pursue” or “be on the lookout”), with the prefix ب- plus the verb پوییدن; کاین is the demonstrative “that/this,” here introducing a clause of explanation; مهتر is “chief/leader”; آهرمن is Ahriman (the devil); است is the copula “is.” The clause states the identification/reason: “for this leader is Ahriman.”
segments.s029-g102: جهان‌آفرین را is topicalized with the object marker را (“the Creator”); به دل دشمن is a locative/genitive phrase “in the heart of the enemy” (به + دل = “in/within the heart of”); است is the copula. Together the clause says that the Creator is (present) as an enemy in that person’s heart—i.e. the man bears hostility toward the Creator in his heart. Note the poetic word order: topicalized object + location + copula.
segments.s029-g103: بدان is the compound of the prefix ب- and دان (literally “know/consider”), functioning here as an imperative/introductory “know this / be aware.” بی‌بها and ناسزاوار are successive modifiers of پوست: both are pejorative epithets (literally “without value / worthless” and “deserving insult / detestable”), and پوست is “skin” (used for the person’s body/appearance). The whole nominal group points at a contemned physical person (“that worthless, detestable skin”).
segments.s029-g104: پدید آمد is a two-word verbal phrase “appeared / arose” (پدید + آمد). آوای دشمن is a genitive construct “the voice/sound of the enemy,” and ز دوست is a prepositional phrase “from a friend” (ز = “from,” poetic form). The clause therefore reports that an enemy’s voice/provocation came out from a friend—i.e. hostile words were spoken by someone who was (supposedly) a friend.
segments.s029-g17: که here introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). The noun دشمن is the clause subject. اگر چه is a concessive pair meaning 'even if/although' (in classical Persian the two words form the idiom), followed by the past copula بود 'was'. The adjectives خوار و خرد follow the noun in the typical Persian order and mean 'despised' (خوار) and 'small/insignificant' (خرد) here — not 'wisdom'.
segments.s029-g18: نبایدت is a negative modal with the enclitic -ت addressing 'you' (classical form meaning 'you must not / it should not be for you'). او را is the 3rd‑person object with the emphatic/definite marker را. به پی is a prepositional phrase 'into/for pursuit' (به + پی 'in/into the chase'), and بر + سپرد form the verbal idea 'to hand over/entrust toward' — سپرد is the past infinitive/stem 'to hand over'.
segments.s029-g19: ندارم is the negated present 'I do not have' (ن + دارم). همی is an archaic/emphatic particle ('indeed/just' or continuous) placed before the noun phrase. The noun phrase دشمن خرد خوار places the modifiers after the noun: خرد = 'small/insignificant' and خوار = 'despised/worthless'.
segments.s029-g20: بترسم is 1st‑person present/future sense formed with the prefix ب‑ + ترسـ root, here 'I fear / I am afraid'. همی again gives continuous/emphatic force. از introduces the source/target of the fear ('of'), followed by بد روزگار 'bad fate/ill fortune' (بد = 'bad', روزگار = 'fate').
segments.s029-g21: همی gives an emphatic/continuous flavour. زین here is the fused/poetic form of 'از این' ('from this / because of this'); its separate lemma senses given in the list (saddle) do not apply, so the sense index is set to -1. فزون means 'greater/more/abundant'. بایدم is the older inflected form of باید with the enclitic -م indicating 'I must' ('I ought'); لشکری is the noun 'army/troops' following the verb phrase.
segments.s029-g22: هم ... هم is the correlative pair 'both ... and'. از (and the poetic variant ز) mark origin/source 'from'. مردم = 'people', دیو = 'demon', پری = 'fairy'; و is the coordinating 'and'. The whole phrase lists the composition of the needed troops: some from humans, some from supernatural beings.
segments.s029-g23: یکی here means 'one' / 'a single' (introducing the unit). لشگری (army) is the object. خواهم is the auxiliary 'I will' or 'I intend', and انگیختن is the infinitive 'to raise/rouse'; together خواهم انگیختن gives a classical future sense 'I will raise'.
segments.s029-g24: ابا is the noun/verb meaning 'reluctance / to be reluctant'. Here it pairs with the following verbal noun برآمیختن ('mixing together') to express 'reluctance about mixing demons and people' — and in context the speaker means he will have no such reluctance (the negation is pragmatic/elliptical). دیو مردم are the two direct objects being mixed (demon + people). برآمیختن is the infinitive 'to mix/mingle'. Grammatically the second infinitive is coordinated with the first (خواهم governs both infinitives).
segments.s029-g169: Poetic inversion: بسی (an intensifier) modifies the verb-phrase کردشان (کرد + شان, “did to them”), with نیز “also” adding that this action is in addition to others; the personal name فرخ (Farrokh) appears after the verb as the subject by poetic word order, and امید is the direct object “hope.” Altogether: “Farrokh also did much for them and gave them hope.”
segments.s029-g170: بسی again intensifies or quantifies دادشان (داد + شان, “gave to them”); مهتری را is the definite object marked by را (the promise pertains to a higher rank/preeminence), and نوید is the thing given — “a promise/good news.” Word order places the intensifier and verb before the object for poetic effect: “He gave them oft/a great promise of preeminence.”
segments.s029-g171: که‌گر is a contracted conditional (که اگر) “if”; اژدها را is the definite object (را marks the dragon as object); کنم is the 1st‑person verb (here conditional/subjunctive ‘I put/do’); زیر خاک is a prepositional phrase “under the earth.” The clause reads: “If I put the dragon under the earth…”
segments.s029-g172: بشویم is a subjunctive/optative 1st‑person form meaning here “I will wash/cleanse” (poetic; subject is omitted); شما را marks “you” as the object; سر is the thing acted on (“head[s]”); از گرد پاک is a phrase “clean of dust” (از = from/off, گرد = dust, پاک = clean). Entire segment: “I will wash/cleanse the dust from your heads.”
segments.s029-g145: برادر دو بودش is an idiomatic word order where the numeral follows the noun and بودش (بود + enclitic ش) functions like 'there was to him' — i.e., 'he had'. دو فرخ همال is a noun phrase in apposition: فرخ (fortunate) modifies همال (here used figuratively as 'peer' or 'equal').
segments.s029-g146: ازو here marks comparison/relation 'than him' (literally 'from him'); هر دو = 'both (the two)'; آزاده is an adjectival quality 'noble/free‑hearted'; مهتر به سال is a comparative phrase where مهتر 'older/elder' takes به سال 'in years' (به used adverbially 'by/—ly').
segments.s029-g147: یکی بود ازیشان = 'one was among them' (یکی + past بود + ازِ ایشان 'of them'); the proper name follows (کیانوش نام) where نام introduces the following as the name of that one — a common poetic word order.
segments.s029-g148: دگر = 'the other'; نام introduces the following words as that person's name; پرمایه شادکام are adjectival/epithetic elements attached to the name (here treated as a compound or honorific name).
segments.s029-g149: فریدون is the subject; بریشان = بر + ایشان 'toward/to them' (prepositional phrase). زبان برگشاد is a poetic nominal+verb collocation 'opened his speech/tongue' (زبان here means 'speech/utterance'), with برگشاد (opened) as the verbal action.
segments.s029-g150: که introduces the quoted or content clause ('that/namely'); خرم زیید is an optative/wish (زیید here = 'live' used like 'may you live') addressed to the audience; ای is the vocative 'O', دلیران is the plural vocative 'brave ones', and و شاد adds 'and happy'.
segments.s029-g151: که again introduces a wish/clause. گردون is the subject 'the heavens/fate'; نگردد is negative subjunctive/optative ('may not turn' or 'may it not come to pass'), here functioning as a wish about fortune. به جز ... = 'except', and بر بهی marks the target 'toward good' (بر + بهی).
segments.s029-g152: This is another optative clause with verb before subject: به ما بازگردد = 'may ... return to us' (بازگردد subjunctive/optative + indirect object به ما). کلاه مهی is the subject in inverted order — 'the moon‑like cap/crown' (مهی 'moon‑like') — which is the thing hoped to return.
segments.s029-g161: نگاری (a noun) is the direct object meaning 'a picture/likeness'; نگارید here functions as a past verbal action 'they painted/drew' — an archaic/poetic form built on نگاریدن. The prepositional phrase بر خاک پیش follows, giving the location 'on the ground in front of…'. Word order places the painted thing first (stylistic inversion) then the verb.
segments.s029-g162: همیدون is a proper name in the prepositional phrase 'in front of Hamīdūn'. به سان is the fixed similative 'like/as', and سر گاومیش is the simile's noun phrase 'the head of a water‑buffalo'. The sequence 'به سان سر گاومیش' modifies the preceding likeness.
segments.s029-g163: بر آن 'at that' / 'on that (occasion)' introduces the circumstance. دست بردند is the past-tense idiom meaning 'they put their hand to it' = 'they set to work' (بردند past plural). آهنگران is the agent 'the blacksmiths' and follows the verb in typical classical word order.
segments.s029-g164: چو شد ساخته is a temporal clause 'when it became made / when it had been made' (چو = 'when', شد + ساخته = result/completion). کار گرز گران is the noun phrase 'the work/making of the heavy mace' (گرز گران = 'heavy mace'). The clause states the time at which the smiths' action is completed.
segments.s029-g165: به پیش + noun is 'before/in the presence of'; here به پیش جهان‌جوی + بردند + گرز = 'they brought/carried the mace before the world‑seeker.' بردند is past plural 'they carried/brought'. The prepositional phrase leads into the verb phrase.
segments.s029-g166: فروزان is an adjective 'shining/radiant' describing the mace; به کردار is a comparative/formal phrase 'in the manner of', and خورشید برز 'the exalted/high sun' is the standard of comparison — together: 'radiant as the exalted sun.' The adjective appears before the comparative phrase in poetic order.
segments.s029-g167: پسند آمدش is an idiom meaning 'it pleased him' ('پسند' = approval/pleasure, آمدش = 'came to him' = 'he liked'). کار پولادگر is the subject of that pleasure: 'the work of the steel‑worker/the smith's work.' The order places the feeling expression first, then the thing causing it.
segments.s029-g168: ببخشیدشان is a past verb meaning here 'he gave/bestowed upon them' (from بخشیدن). جامه و سیم و زر is the list of gifts 'garments, silver, and gold' coordinated by و 'and'. Note: ببخشیدشان can also mean 'forgave them' in other contexts; here it functions as 'bestowed'.
segments.s029-g65: This clause uses a past tense transitive verb نهادید (past, 3rd-plural) with an implicit subject 'they/people'. Word order is topical: همه سوی دوزخ (everyone toward hell) precedes the verb نهادید. روی here is a noun 'face' (object of نهادید) and the phrase means 'set/turned their faces toward hell.' Poetic inversion omits an explicit subject.
segments.s029-g66: سپردید is past (3rd-plural) 'they entrusted/handed over' and takes دل‌ها as its direct object. The preposition به marks the indirect object 'to' (به گفتار اوی = 'to his speech/words'). اوی is the archaic/enclitic possessive form 'his/her'. The clause continues the same subject from the previous verb.
segments.s029-g67: نباشم is first‑person negative (classical/subjunctive/optative flavor) 'I would not be / let me not be'. بدین here is the fused form of به + این ('to / in this'), split in the word list as ب + دین; together they form بدین 'in/at this'. محضر 'presence/court' is the place, اندر 'in/within' is a locative particle, and گوا here means 'witness'. The phrase reads idiomatically 'I should not be in this court as a witness.'
segments.s029-g68: نه هرگز is an emphatic negative 'not ever / never'. براندیشم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'I consider / I reflect' (here negative, 'I will not reflect'). از introduces the topic/target of thought ('concerning / about') and پادشا is 'the king'. Together: 'I will never think/contemplate concerning the king.'
segments.s029-g69: This clause strings actions: خروشید (past, 3rd-plural) 'they roared' followed by و (and) then برجست 'rose up/appeared/started up' — here physical motion 'sprang up'. لرزان is a participial adjective 'trembling' modifying the implied actors; ز جای 'from their places' gives the origin. Poetic word order places manner and origin before/after the verbs.
segments.s029-g70: بدرید is past plural 'they tore' (likely garments). و (and) links to بسپرد, here past 'they threw/surrendered/committed' (بسپرد used transitively), with محضر 'the presence/court' as the place/scene and به پای 'at the feet' indicating they cast themselves down or presented something at the feet — idiomatically 'threw themselves at the feet in the court.'
segments.s029-g71: گرانمایه is an attributive adjective 'precious/esteemed'. فرزند او is 'his son' (owner specified by او). پیش اوی places the son 'before him / in his presence' — پیش as preposition 'before, in the presence of' and اوی is the archaic possessive/enclitic 'his'. The word order places the epithet and relationship before the location phrase.
segments.s029-g72: ز ایوان برون شد combines the preposition ز 'from' + ایوان 'iwan/porch' and the compound برون شد 'went out/issued forth' (شد here in a stative/motion sense 'became / went out'). خروشان is adverbial/participial 'roaring, tumultuously', and به کوی 'into the street/quarter' marks the destination. The sequence gives motion out of the iwan into the street.
segments.s029-g153: بیارید is a 2nd-person plural imperative ‘bring’ (a command addressed to more than one addressee). داننده is a participial/adjectival noun ‘knowledgeable/expert’ that qualifies آهنگران; together داننده آهنگران means ‘the skilled ones among the blacksmiths’ (literally ‘knowers of the smiths’), a poetic compound. The phrase is the direct object of the imperative.
segments.s029-g154: یکی is an indefinite numeral/adjective ‘one’ or ‘a certain’. گرز is the noun ‘mace’ (the thing to be made). فرمود is a past reporting verb 'he said/commanded' used here to introduce what was ordered. باید is a modal ‘must/ought to’ and governs the following adjective گران ‘heavy’ — Persian drops the copula, so باید + adjective = ‘must be [adjective]’. Together: ‘He ordered one mace — it must be heavy.’
segments.s029-g155: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بگشاد is a past form 'opened' (here of لب 'lips/mouths'); the verb appears before or near the noun in the subordinate temporal clause. هر دو 'both' specifies that the two persons acted together. بشتافتند is the main verb of the sentence 'they hurried/rushed' (3rd-person plural past), so the subordinate clause introduced by چو sets the time: 'When both opened their mouths, they hurried.'
segments.s029-g156: به is the preposition 'toward/into'. بازار 'market' is the place, and آهنگران specifies which market ('the blacksmiths' market'). تاختند is a past 3rd-person plural verb from تاختن meaning 'they rushed' or 'they charged'; here context makes 'they hurried/rushed (to the market)' the intended sense. The whole phrase states the destination and action: 'they rushed to the blacksmiths' market.'
segments.s029-g157: هر آن کس is a standard relative phrase 'anyone / every person who'. کز is a relative particle 'who/that/from which' linking the relative clause. آن پیشه 'that trade/craft' is the source or domain, and بد نام‌جوی is a compound where بد ('bad') qualifies نام‌جوی 'fame-seeker' — together meaning someone who seeks (ill) fame by that craft or simply 'one who seeks fame through that trade'. The whole segment builds the noun phrase 'anyone who sought fame by that craft.'
segments.s029-g158: به سوی is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward'. فریدون is the proper name (the addressee of the motion). نهادند is the past 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'to place/set' used idiomatically with روی ('face') — نهادند روی = 'they set/placed their face' = 'they turned their faces'. روی here denotes 'face' (direction). So the clause means 'they turned their faces toward Fereydun.'
segments.s029-g159: جهان‌جوی is an epithet/noun 'world-seeker' (the person performing the action). پرگار is the direct object 'compass' (drawing instrument). بگرفت is a past tense verb 'he seized/took up'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the verb; placed after the verb here for emphasis. The clause states the subject's swift action: 'the world-seeker quickly took hold of the compass.'
segments.s029-g160: وزان is a connective adverb 'and from that / from it' linking this action to the previous one. گرز پیکر is a noun phrase in which گرز 'mace' is qualified by پیکر 'form/figure' = 'the form of the mace' (i.e., the mace-shaped form or dimensions). بدیشان is a compound prepositional/object form 'to them' (ب + ایشان). نمود is the past 3rd-person singular of نمودن/نمود 'he showed / made appear' — so the clause means 'from that he showed them the mace's form.'
